reasoning:
A formal and legal ban on a political party in Germany can legally only be issued by the Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht). In the history of the Federal Republic, only two parties have been banned (SRP in 1952 and KPD in 1956). Modern attempts to ban the NPD took several years each (2001-2003 and 2013-2017).

For the AfD, a cross-party motion to apply for a ban was debated in the Bundestag on January 30, 2025. However, there was no vote, because there was no majority. Furthermore, the German federal elections were scheduled for February 2025, and there is an understanding that passing such a motion in the new Bundestag will be even harder due to the AfD's projected strength. 

Even if the Bundesrat, Bundestag, or Federal Government were to officially file an application to ban the AfD tomorrow, the Constitutional Court would require extensive evidentiary hearings, expert testimonies, and deliberations. It is completely unprecedented and structurally impossible for the Court to process and rule on banning a major political party in fewer than 6 months. Therefore, an official announcement of a ban on the AfD by July 1, 2025, has a probability practically indistinguishable from zero.
